seoTitle: Injoinic Technical Support - Fast Charging & Power Management Resources
seoDescription: Access Injoinic technical documentation, application notes, selection guides, and FAE support. LiTong provides comprehensive technical resources for power management designs.

faqs.question: What technical resources does LiTong provide for Injoinic products?
faqs.answer: LiTong provides comprehensive technical resources for Injoinic products including datasheets, application notes, reference designs, selection guides, and evaluation boards. Our FAE team offers design review services, schematic and layout guidance, debugging support, and application-specific recommendations. We also provide sample kits for evaluation and can arrange factory training sessions for high-volume customers.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our technical support team for access to documentation and design assistance. We can provide samples for evaluation.

faqs.question: How can I get samples of Injoinic products?
faqs.answer: Samples of Injoinic products are available through LiTong for qualified customers. To request samples, contact our sales team with your company information, project details, and required quantities. We typically provide small quantities (5-10 pieces) for evaluation purposes. For larger sample requirements or production ramp-up, we can discuss your forecast and schedule. Sample lead time is typically 1-2 weeks for standard products.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our sales team with your project details to request samples. Provide as much application information as possible.

faqs.question: Does LiTong provide schematic and layout review services?
faqs.answer: Yes, LiTong provides complimentary schematic and PCB layout review services for designs using Injoinic products. Our FAE team reviews your design for proper component selection, circuit configuration, thermal management, and EMI considerations. We provide detailed feedback with recommendations for optimization. This service helps catch potential issues early, reducing debug time and improving design reliability.
faqs.decisionGuide: Submit your schematic and layout files to our FAE team for review. Early review is recommended before PCB fabrication.

faqs.question: What is the typical response time for technical inquiries?
faqs.answer: LiTong aims to respond to technical inquiries within 24 hours for standard questions. Complex design issues or questions requiring factory consultation may take 2-3 business days. For urgent production issues, we provide priority support with faster response times. Our FAE team is available during business hours (9 AM - 6 PM China time) for phone or video consultation.
faqs.decisionGuide: For urgent issues, contact us by phone. For detailed technical questions, email with supporting documentation.

faqs.question: Can LiTong help with design troubleshooting?
faqs.answer: Yes, LiTong provides troubleshooting support for designs using Injoinic products. Our FAE team can help diagnose issues through schematic review, waveform analysis, and remote debugging. For complex issues, we can coordinate with Injoinic factory engineers for deeper analysis. Common support areas include protocol detection problems, power conversion efficiency issues, thermal management, and EMI debugging.
faqs.decisionGuide: For troubleshooting, provide detailed problem description, schematic, and measurement data. Contact our FAE team for assistance.

faqs.question: Does LiTong provide training on Injoinic products?
faqs.answer: LiTong offers product training sessions for customers using Injoinic products. Training topics include product overview, application design guidelines, troubleshooting techniques, and hands-on workshops. Training can be conducted at your facility, our offices, or via web conference. For high-volume customers, we can arrange customized training programs tailored to your specific applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us to schedule training sessions. We can customize content for your team's needs.

faqs.question: Where can I find the latest datasheets and application notes?
faqs.answer: The latest Injoinic datasheets and application notes are available from LiTong. Contact our sales or technical support team to request documentation. We maintain the most current versions direct from the factory. Documentation packages typically include datasheets, application notes, reference design files, and software tools. We can also provide preliminary documentation for upcoming products under NDA.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our team to request documentation. Specify the products you're interested in.

faqs.question: What information should I provide when requesting technical support?
faqs.answer: When requesting technical support, please provide: detailed problem description, product part numbers, schematic and PCB layout files, bill of materials, measurement data or waveforms, operating conditions (voltage, temperature, load), and any error messages or symptoms observed. The more information you provide, the faster we can diagnose and resolve your issue.
faqs.decisionGuide: Gather all relevant documentation before contacting support. Detailed information leads to faster resolution.

articles.title: Fast Charging Protocol IC Selection Guide
articles.summary: Comprehensive guide for selecting the right Injoinic fast charging protocol IC based on application requirements, protocol support needs, and design constraints.

author.name: David Chen
author.title: Senior FAE - Power Solutions
author.bio: David has over 15 years of experience in power electronics and fast charging technologies, supporting hundreds of successful customer designs.

content: Selecting the right fast charging protocol IC is critical for the success of your charging product. This guide provides a systematic approach to choosing the optimal Injoinic solution for your application.
The first step is identifying which fast charging protocols your product needs to support:
- **USB Power Delivery (PD)**: Essential for laptops, tablets, and modern smartphones. Supports up to 100W+ with PPS for precise voltage control.
- **Qualcomm Quick Charge (QC)**: Widely used in Android smartphones. QC 3.0 and 4.0+ provide backward compatibility and fast charging.
- **Huawei SCP/FCP**: Required for Huawei devices. SCP supports up to 40W+ charging.
- **Samsung AFC**: Used in Samsung Galaxy devices. Supports up to 25W.
- **MediaTek PE**: Found in phones using MediaTek processors.
For universal compatibility, select an IC supporting all major protocols like the IP2723T or IP2726.
- **5-18W**: Suitable for smartphones and small tablets. Standard power bank range.
- **20-30W**: Fast charging for large smartphones and tablets.
Ensure your power stage design can support the required output current with adequate thermal management.
- **Single Port**: Simplest design, lowest cost. IP2723T is ideal.
- **Dual Port**: Requires intelligent power sharing. IP2726 provides dual-port control.
- **Multi-Port**: May require multiple ICs or more complex power management architecture.
| Protocols | All major | All major | PD/QC |
- **Single-port power banks**: IP2723T for universal compatibility up to 100W
- **Dual-port power banks**: IP2726 with intelligent power sharing
- **Budget power banks**: IP2721 for basic PD/QC support up to 18W
Pair with IP6538 buck-boost converter for single-cell battery applications.
- **Single-port chargers**: IP2723T with external power stage
- **Multi-port chargers**: IP2726 or multiple IP2723T with external power management
- **High-density designs**: Consider thermal management and efficiency optimization
- Wide input voltage range (9V-16V typical, load dump protection needed)
IP2723T is suitable with proper input protection and thermal design.
Proper layout is critical for reliable operation:
1. Place decoupling capacitors close to power pins
2. Route CC lines carefully with proper impedance
4. Provide adequate copper for heat dissipation

Selecting the right fast charging IC requires careful consideration of protocol requirements, power levels, and application constraints. Injoinic's product portfolio offers solutions for virtually any application, from simple chargers to complex multi-port systems.
Contact LiTong's FAE team for personalized recommendations based on your specific requirements.

faeInsights.insight: The most common mistake I see in fast charging design is underestimating the importance of CC line layout. The CC (Configuration Channel) lines are critical for USB PD communication - they carry the protocol negotiation signals that determine charging voltage and current. Poor layout can result in intermittent charging, failed protocol detection, or compatibility issues with certain devices. Always route CC lines as differential pairs with controlled impedance, keep them away from switching noise sources, and use appropriate pull-up resistors. Another critical factor is power path design - the MOSFETs used for power switching must have low enough RDS(on) to minimize voltage drop and heating at maximum current. I typically recommend MOSFETs with RDS(on) below 10mΩ for 3A+ applications.
faeInsights.logic: The selection process should follow this decision tree: First, determine if you need single or multiple ports - this immediately narrows the product selection. Second, identify the maximum power level required based on your target devices. Third, list the protocols you must support for compatibility. Fourth, consider integration level versus flexibility - higher integration reduces BOM but may limit customization. Fifth, evaluate thermal requirements based on power level and enclosure constraints. Finally, consider cost targets and production volume to optimize the solution.
keyTakeaways: CC line layout is critical for reliable PD operation
Power path MOSFET selection affects efficiency and thermal performance
Protocol support should match your target device ecosystem
Thermal design must account for worst-case continuous operation
Higher integration reduces BOM cost but verify all required features are included
commonPitfalls: Inadequate CC line routing causing protocol detection failures
Undersized power path components leading to excessive heating
Insufficient protocol support limiting device compatibility
Poor thermal design causing derating under load
Inadequate input protection for the application environment
bestPractices: Use differential routing for CC lines with proper impedance control
Select power MOSFETs with RDS(on) appropriate for your maximum current
Implement comprehensive protocol support for universal compatibility
Design thermal management for worst-case continuous operation
Include adequate protection for your application environment
faeInsights.content: Based on years of experience, this Fast Charging Protocol IC Selection Guide article addresses common challenges engineers face in project development.
faeInsights.insightLogic: Recommendations from analysis of successful product deployment projects across industries.

customerCases.problem: The customer was developing their first power bank product and was overwhelmed by the various fast charging protocols and IC options. They needed guidance on selecting the right solution for a dual-port 30W power bank targeting the mid-range market.
customerCases.diagnosis: After reviewing their requirements, we determined they needed multi-protocol support for universal compatibility and dual-port operation with power sharing. Their cost target required a balance between features and integration.
customerCases.solution: We recommended the IP2726 dual-port controller paired with a high-efficiency buck-boost converter. Our FAE team provided detailed guidance on protocol selection, schematic design, and layout optimization. We also provided reference designs and sample code.
results: Successfully launched product with 30W dual-port output
Achieved compatibility with 95% of tested devices
Passed all safety certifications on first attempt
Product achieved 4.3-star rating on major platforms
Customer placed follow-on orders for additional models
customerCases.customerFeedback: LiTong's guidance was invaluable for our first power bank design. The IC selection recommendations were spot-on, and the technical support helped us avoid common pitfalls. We couldn't have achieved this success without their expertise.

faqs.question: How do I choose between IP2723T and IP2726?
faqs.answer: Choose IP2723T for single-port applications where you need a compact, cost-effective solution. Select IP2726 for dual-port designs requiring intelligent power sharing between ports. The IP2726 integrates power allocation logic that would otherwise require external components and complex firmware when using two single-port ICs.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use IP2723T for single port, IP2726 for dual port with power sharing. Contact us for multi-port designs requiring more than two ports.

faqs.question: What is the difference between PD 3.0 and PD 3.1?
faqs.answer: PD 3.1 extends the power range up to 240W (48V/5A) compared to PD 3.0's 100W (20V/5A) limit. PD 3.1 also introduces Extended Power Range (EPR) with new voltage levels (28V, 36V, 48V). For most consumer applications, PD 3.0's 100W is sufficient. PD 3.1 is primarily for high-power applications like gaming laptops and professional equipment. Both versions support PPS (Programmable Power Supply) for precise voltage control.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use PD 3.0 for most applications up to 100W. Consider PD 3.1 only for high-power applications exceeding 100W.

faqs.question: Do I need to support all fast charging protocols?
faqs.answer: Supporting all protocols provides maximum device compatibility but increases cost and complexity. For many applications, supporting PD and QC covers 80-90% of devices. Consider your target market - if targeting Huawei or Samsung users, add SCP or AFC support. For premium universal products, support all major protocols. For cost-sensitive products, PD-only may be sufficient for the premium segment.
faqs.decisionGuide: Analyze your target market to determine required protocols. We can provide market data on protocol prevalence by region and device type.

faqs.question: What power level should I target for my application?
faqs.answer: Power level selection depends on your target devices: 18W covers most smartphones with basic fast charging; 30W provides faster charging for large phones and small tablets; 45-65W supports tablets and laptops; 100W+ is for high-performance laptops. Consider your product positioning - premium products should offer higher power, while budget products can target lower power levels. Also consider thermal constraints - higher power requires better thermal management.
faqs.decisionGuide: Match power level to your target devices and product positioning. Consider thermal constraints for higher power designs.

faqs.question: How important is PPS (Programmable Power Supply) support?
faqs.answer: PPS is increasingly important for newer smartphones from Samsung, Google, and others. It allows precise voltage adjustment (20mV steps) for optimal charging efficiency and battery health. PPS can provide faster charging with less heat generation compared to fixed voltage levels. For premium products targeting flagship phones, PPS support is highly recommended. For basic products, it may be optional depending on target market.
faqs.decisionGuide: Include PPS for premium products targeting flagship smartphones. It may be optional for budget or basic products.

faqs.question: What thermal considerations are important for protocol ICs?
faqs.answer: Protocol ICs themselves typically have low power dissipation, but the overall system thermal design is critical. The power path components (MOSFETs) generate most heat. Ensure adequate copper area for heat spreading, consider thermal vias to inner layers, and design for worst-case continuous operation. High ambient temperatures (like in car chargers) require additional thermal margin. Monitor temperature during testing and implement derating if necessary.
faqs.decisionGuide: Focus thermal design on power path components. Test under worst-case conditions including high ambient temperature.

articles.title: Power Bank Design Guide with Injoinic Solutions
articles.summary: Comprehensive guide for designing power banks using Injoinic fast charging protocol ICs and power management solutions.

author.name: Michael Wang
author.title: FAE Manager - Consumer Electronics
author.bio: Michael specializes in portable power solutions with over 12 years of experience in power bank and battery-powered product design.

content: Power bank design requires careful integration of battery management, power conversion, and fast charging protocols. This guide covers best practices for designing power banks using Injoinic solutions.
A typical power bank system consists of:
1. **Battery Pack**: Single or multiple lithium cells with protection
2. **Charging Circuit**: Manages battery charging from input
4. **Protocol IC**: Handles fast charging protocol negotiation
6. **Control Logic**: Manages power flow and user interface
Choose between cylindrical cells (18650, 21700) or polymer cells:
- **18650 Cells**: Standardized, readily available, good for replaceable designs
- **21700 Cells**: Higher capacity, better for high-power applications
- **Polymer Cells**: Customizable shape, thin profiles possible
Output Capacity = Battery Capacity × Voltage Ratio × Efficiency
Account for conversion efficiency (typically 85-95%) in your specifications.
For power banks with fast input charging:
- Use dedicated battery charger IC or integrated solution
Use dedicated protection IC like IP3005 for single-cell designs.
Choose converter topology based on requirements:
- **Boost Only**: Simple, lower cost, fixed 5V output
- **Buck-Boost**: Flexible output voltage, supports fast charging protocols
For multi-protocol fast charging, buck-boost topology (IP6538) is recommended.
Select protocol IC based on port configuration:
- **Dual Port**: IP2726 with intelligent power sharing
- Proper CC pull-up resistor values (5.1kΩ for default, others for current advertisement)
- CC line routing with appropriate impedance
- Proper handling of CC line orientation detection
- ESD protection on all external ports
- Monitor temperature at battery and power components
- **Single LED**: Different blink patterns for status
- **Button Press**: User presses button to check status or activate output
- **Auto-Detect**: Output activates when device is connected
- Easy access for assembly and testing
- Input charging at various voltage levels
- **Safety**: UL, CE, CCC depending on markets
Successful power bank design requires attention to electrical, thermal, and mechanical aspects. Injoinic's integrated solutions simplify design while providing excellent performance and reliability.
Contact LiTong's FAE team for design review and optimization recommendations.

faeInsights.insight: The most critical aspect of power bank design that I see overlooked is the power path management during pass-through charging. When a power bank is being charged while simultaneously charging a connected device, the power flow must be carefully managed to prioritize device charging while safely charging the internal battery. The IP6538 handles this well, but the control logic and MOSFET selection for the power path are crucial. Use low-RDS(on) MOSFETs for minimal voltage drop and heating. Another common issue is inadequate protection - always use a dedicated battery protection IC like IP3005 in addition to any protection integrated in the converter. The cost savings of omitting protection are not worth the safety risk.
faeInsights.logic: Power bank design follows a systematic approach: First, define your capacity and power requirements based on target devices and use cases. Second, select the battery configuration (single cell vs. multi-cell) based on capacity and voltage requirements. Third, choose the converter topology - buck-boost is most flexible for protocol support. Fourth, select the protocol IC based on required charging standards and port configuration. Fifth, design comprehensive protection including battery, thermal, and fault protection. Sixth, optimize for efficiency and thermal performance. Finally, validate through extensive testing including protocol compatibility and safety verification.
keyTakeaways: Always use dedicated battery protection IC in addition to converter protection
Power path design is critical for pass-through charging functionality
Calculate actual output capacity accounting for conversion efficiency
Thermal design must consider worst-case continuous operation
Protocol compatibility testing with real devices is essential
commonPitfalls: Insufficient battery protection compromising safety
Inadequate power path design causing voltage drop or heating
Overstated capacity specifications not accounting for efficiency
Poor thermal design leading to derating or shutdown
Inadequate protocol testing resulting in device compatibility issues
bestPractices: Include dedicated battery protection IC in all designs
Design power path with low-RDS(on) MOSFETs for minimal losses
Calculate and specify realistic output capacity
Implement comprehensive thermal management with monitoring
Conduct thorough protocol compatibility testing
faeInsights.content: Based on years of experience, this Power Bank Design Guide with Injoinic Solutions article addresses common challenges engineers face in project development.
faeInsights.insightLogic: Recommendations from analysis of successful product deployment projects across industries.

customerCases.problem: The customer was designing a high-capacity power bank and encountered thermal issues during fast charging. The device would overheat and throttle output power after several minutes of operation.
customerCases.diagnosis: Analysis revealed insufficient thermal management design. The PCB had inadequate copper area for heat spreading, and the enclosure lacked ventilation. The MOSFETs were operating near their thermal limits.
customerCases.solution: We recommended a redesign with improved thermal management: increased copper pours with thermal vias, better MOSFET selection with lower RDS(on), and enclosure modifications for improved airflow. Our FAE team provided detailed layout guidance.
results: Reduced operating temperature by 15°C
Achieved continuous 18W output without throttling
Improved efficiency from 88% to 93%
Product passed all thermal safety tests
Customer expanded product line with confidence
customerCases.customerFeedback: LiTong's thermal design expertise solved our critical issue. The detailed guidance on PCB layout and component selection made a significant difference. Our product now performs reliably even under heavy load.

faqs.question: What is the typical efficiency of a power bank?
faqs.answer: Typical power bank efficiency ranges from 85% to 95% depending on design quality. This includes both charging efficiency (input to battery) and discharging efficiency (battery to output). High-quality designs using synchronous rectification and proper component selection achieve 90-95% efficiency. Budget designs may be in the 85-88% range. When calculating usable output capacity, multiply battery capacity by efficiency (typically 0.85-0.90).
faqs.decisionGuide: Target 90%+ efficiency for premium products. Use synchronous converters and quality components for best efficiency.

faqs.question: How do I calculate the actual output capacity?
faqs.answer: Calculate actual output capacity using: Output Capacity = Battery Capacity × (Battery Voltage / Output Voltage) × Efficiency. For example, a 10000mAh battery at 3.7V converting to 5V at 90% efficiency: 10000mAh × (3.7/5) × 0.90 = 6660mAh at 5V. Always specify capacity at rated output voltage, not battery voltage, to avoid misleading specifications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate and specify realistic output capacity. Account for voltage conversion and efficiency losses.

faqs.question: What battery protection is required?
faqs.answer: Power banks require comprehensive battery protection including: over-charge protection (typically 4.25-4.35V), over-discharge protection (2.5-2.8V), over-current protection, short-circuit protection, and temperature protection. Use a dedicated battery protection IC like IP3005 for single-cell designs. Never rely solely on converter-integrated protection - dedicated protection ICs provide more reliable and faster response.
faqs.decisionGuide: Always use dedicated battery protection IC. IP3005 is recommended for single-cell designs.

faqs.question: How do I implement pass-through charging?
faqs.answer: Pass-through charging requires proper power path management. When input power is present, the system should charge the battery while simultaneously powering the output. Use appropriate power path MOSFETs and control logic. The IP6538 supports pass-through with proper configuration. Ensure the input power source can supply both battery charging current and output load current simultaneously.
faqs.decisionGuide: Design power path with adequate current capacity. Test pass-through operation under maximum load conditions.

faqs.question: What is the best topology for fast charging power banks?
faqs.answer: Buck-boost topology is best for fast charging power banks as it can maintain regulated output voltage regardless of battery voltage. As the battery discharges from 4.2V to 3.0V, a buck-boost converter maintains stable 5V, 9V, or 12V output. Boost-only converters can only output voltages higher than battery voltage, limiting fast charging capability when battery is low.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use buck-boost topology for multi-voltage fast charging. IP6538 is recommended for this application.

faqs.question: How do I design for safety certifications?
faqs.answer: Design for safety certifications from the start: Use certified battery cells with proper protection, implement comprehensive protection circuits, design adequate clearances and creepage distances, use flame-retardant materials, implement temperature monitoring and protection, and design for abuse conditions (short circuit, overcharge). Work with a certified testing lab early to identify any issues. Common certifications include UL, CE, CCC, and UN38.3 for battery transportation.
faqs.decisionGuide: Plan for certifications early in design. Engage with testing labs during development phase.

articles.title: Battery Management Design Guide
articles.summary: Comprehensive guide for designing battery management and protection systems using Injoinic battery management ICs.

author.name: Tom Li
author.title: FAE - Battery Solutions
author.bio: Tom specializes in battery management systems with extensive experience in lithium battery protection and monitoring solutions.

content: Proper battery management is essential for safety, performance, and longevity of lithium battery systems. This guide covers battery protection design using Injoinic solutions.
Understanding lithium battery behavior is crucial:
- Short circuit (can cause fire or explosion)
For single-cell applications, the IP3005 provides:
For 2-3 cell series configurations, the IP3008 offers:
Use 1% tolerance resistors for accurate protection.
For protection circuits using external MOSFETs:
- **RDS(on)**: Low as possible to minimize voltage drop and heating
- **ID**: Higher than maximum current with margin
- Cells can become unbalanced due to manufacturing variations
- Different self-discharge rates cause voltage divergence
- Unbalanced cells limit pack capacity and lifetime
- Bleeds excess charge from high cells through resistors
- More efficient but complex and expensive
- Used in high-value or large systems
The IP3008 includes integrated passive balancing:
- Activates when cell voltage exceeds threshold
- **10kΩ at 25°C**: Most common for battery applications
Parallel cells increase capacity; series cells increase voltage.
- Use cells from same batch for better matching
- Match cell capacity and internal resistance
- Implement proper cell connections with adequate current capacity
- Include cell tap connections for monitoring
- Check for over-charge or fault condition
Proper battery management is critical for safe and reliable operation. Injoinic's protection ICs provide comprehensive features for single and multi-cell applications.
Contact LiTong for design support and component selection guidance.

faeInsights.insight: The most critical aspect of battery protection design that I consistently emphasize is the importance of using dedicated protection ICs rather than relying on integrated protection in power converters. While many DC-DC converters include some protection features, they are not a substitute for dedicated battery protection. The response time, accuracy, and reliability of dedicated protection ICs like IP3005 are specifically designed for battery safety. Another common issue I see is inadequate consideration of protection recovery behavior - designers need to understand how and when protection circuits reset after a fault condition. Some protections latch until power is cycled, while others auto-recover when the fault is removed. This behavior must align with your product's use cases and user experience expectations.
faeInsights.logic: Battery protection design follows a safety-first approach: First, identify all potential fault conditions including electrical, thermal, and mechanical abuses. Second, determine appropriate protection thresholds based on cell specifications and application requirements. Third, select protection ICs with suitable features and response characteristics. Fourth, design the protection circuit including current sense, MOSFETs, and any external components. Fifth, implement temperature monitoring with appropriate sensor placement. Sixth, verify protection functionality through comprehensive testing including abuse scenarios. Finally, document all protection parameters and test results for certification.
keyTakeaways: Always use dedicated battery protection ICs, never rely solely on converter protection
Understand protection recovery behavior for each protection function
Match protection thresholds to specific cell specifications
Include temperature monitoring with appropriate thresholds
Test all protection functions including abuse scenarios
commonPitfalls: Relying on converter-integrated protection instead of dedicated protection IC
Not understanding protection recovery behavior causing user confusion
Protection thresholds not matched to cell specifications
Inadequate current sense resistor power rating
Missing temperature monitoring or inappropriate thresholds
bestPractices: Use dedicated protection ICs for all lithium battery applications
Document and test protection recovery behavior
Set protection thresholds based on cell manufacturer specifications
Include adequate safety margin in protection thresholds
Implement comprehensive temperature monitoring
faeInsights.content: Based on years of experience, this Battery Management Design Guide article addresses common challenges engineers face in project development.
faeInsights.insightLogic: Recommendations from analysis of successful product deployment projects across industries.

customerCases.problem: The customer experienced battery pack failures in the field with cells becoming unbalanced, leading to reduced runtime and premature failure. Their existing protection circuit lacked balancing functionality.
customerCases.diagnosis: Analysis showed significant cell voltage divergence after multiple charge cycles. Without balancing, the weaker cell was being over-discharged while the stronger cell remained underutilized.
customerCases.solution: We recommended the IP3008 protection IC with integrated cell balancing. Our FAE team provided guidance on balancing circuit design and cell matching procedures. We also recommended improved cell selection criteria.
results: Cell voltage mismatch reduced from 200mV to <50mV
Pack capacity increased by 15% due to better cell utilization
Cycle life improved from 300 to 500+ cycles
Field failure rate dropped by 90%
Customer expanded product line with confidence
customerCases.customerFeedback: The cell balancing solution transformed our product reliability. LiTong's expertise in battery management helped us understand the importance of balancing in series configurations. Our customers now experience consistent performance throughout the product lifetime.

faqs.question: Why is dedicated battery protection necessary?
faqs.answer: Dedicated battery protection ICs are specifically designed for lithium battery safety with fast response times, accurate thresholds, and reliable operation. While some power converters include basic protection, they are not designed as primary safety mechanisms. Dedicated protection ICs monitor cell voltage with high accuracy, respond to faults in microseconds, and provide multiple protection layers. They are essential for safety certifications and product liability protection. Never rely solely on converter-integrated protection for lithium batteries.
faqs.decisionGuide: Always include dedicated protection IC in battery-powered designs. IP3005 for single cell, IP3008 for 2-3S configurations.

faqs.question: How do I calculate the current sense resistor?
faqs.answer: Calculate sense resistor value using R = Vdetection / Itrip, where Vdetection is the protection IC's over-current detection voltage (typically 100-200mV) and Itrip is your desired protection current. For example, for 3A protection with 150mV detection: R = 0.15V / 3A = 50mΩ. Calculate power dissipation: P = I² × R = 9 × 0.05 = 0.45W. Select a resistor with at least 2x power rating (use 1W or higher). Use 1% tolerance resistors for accurate protection thresholds.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate based on your maximum current requirements. Include adequate safety margin in protection threshold.

faqs.question: What is cell balancing and when is it needed?
faqs.answer: Cell balancing equalizes voltage across series-connected cells. It's essential for multi-cell packs (2S and above) because cells naturally develop different charge levels over time. Without balancing, the pack capacity is limited by the weakest cell, and cells can be over-charged or over-discharged. Passive balancing bleeds excess charge from high cells during charging. Active balancing transfers charge between cells. For most consumer applications, passive balancing is sufficient and more cost-effective.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use balancing for all 2S and higher configurations. IP3008 includes integrated passive balancing.

faqs.question: How do I select protection thresholds?
faqs.answer: Set protection thresholds based on cell manufacturer specifications with safety margin: Over-charge: 4.25-4.30V (slightly below cell limit of 4.35V); Over-discharge: 2.50-2.80V (above cell damage threshold of 2.0V); Over-current: Based on application maximum current plus 20-50% margin; Short-circuit: Typically 2-3x normal maximum current. Temperature thresholds: Charge disable at 0°C and 45°C; Discharge disable at -20°C and 60°C. Always consult cell datasheet and include safety margin.
faqs.decisionGuide: Base thresholds on cell specifications with safety margin. Document rationale for certification.

faqs.question: What temperature monitoring is required?
faqs.answer: Battery packs should include at least one NTC thermistor for temperature monitoring. Place the sensor near the cells for accurate temperature measurement. Typical protection thresholds: Disable charging below 0°C or above 45°C; Disable discharging below -20°C or above 60°C; Absolute shutdown above 75°C. Some applications may require multiple sensors for large packs. The IP3008 supports external NTC connection for temperature monitoring.
faqs.decisionGuide: Include NTC thermistor for all lithium battery packs. Position near cells for accurate measurement.

faqs.question: How do protection circuits recover after a fault?
faqs.answer: Recovery behavior varies by protection type: Over-charge protection typically recovers when voltage drops below release threshold (by connecting a load); Over-discharge protection recovers when charger is connected and voltage rises; Over-current protection may latch (requires power cycle) or auto-recover when load is removed; Short-circuit protection typically latches for safety. Check your protection IC datasheet for specific recovery behavior. Design your product to handle recovery appropriately - some may require user intervention.
faqs.decisionGuide: Understand recovery behavior for each protection function. Design user experience accordingly.

articles.title: Wireless Charging Design Guide
articles.summary: Comprehensive guide for designing Qi-compliant wireless charging solutions using Injoinic transmitter and receiver ICs.

author.name: Alex Huang
author.title: Senior FAE - Wireless Power
author.bio: Alex specializes in wireless power solutions with extensive experience in Qi standard implementation and certification.

content: Wireless charging provides convenience and reliability for powering mobile devices. This guide covers Qi-compliant wireless charging design using Injoinic solutions.
Qi defines two main power profiles:
1. Transmitter coil generates alternating magnetic field
2. Receiver coil converts magnetic field to electrical current
3. Power transfer occurs through air gap between coils
The transmitter coil is critical for performance:
- Q factor: Higher is better for efficiency
- Heat up due to eddy currents (safety hazard)
- Adequate copper area for heat spreading
- Magnetic field coupling to nearby circuits
3. Submit to WPC authorized test lab
- Place coil connections close to power stage
- Use adequate copper for current carrying
- **Capacitors**: High-Q, low ESR for resonant tank

Successful wireless charging design requires attention to coil design, power stage efficiency, FOD reliability, and EMI compliance. Injoinic's IP6808 and IP6806 provide integrated solutions for transmitter and receiver applications.
Contact LiTong for design support and Qi certification guidance.

faeInsights.insight: The most challenging aspect of wireless charging design is achieving consistent FOD (Foreign Object Detection) performance across different operating conditions. FOD calibration must account for your specific coil design, mechanical construction, and expected foreign objects. I've seen designs that work perfectly in the lab but fail in real-world scenarios because they weren't tested with the full range of objects users might place on the charger. Test with coins (various denominations), keys, credit cards, metal phone cases, and even foil-wrapped items. Also consider that FOD sensitivity may need adjustment based on your product's enclosure material and thickness. The IP6808 provides configurable FOD parameters - take advantage of this flexibility to optimize for your specific design.
faeInsights.logic: Wireless charging design follows an iterative optimization process: First, design the coil and resonant tank to meet Qi specifications for inductance and Q factor. Second, implement the power stage with adequate margin for your target power level. Third, establish basic power transfer functionality. Fourth, implement and calibrate FOD for your specific mechanical design. Fifth, optimize efficiency through component selection and layout improvements. Sixth, address EMI through filtering and shielding. Seventh, validate through extensive testing including interoperability with various devices. Finally, prepare for Qi certification with pre-testing using validation equipment.
keyTakeaways: FOD calibration is critical and must be validated with comprehensive object testing
Coil Q factor significantly impacts efficiency - invest in quality coil construction
Qi certification requires thorough preparation and pre-testing
EMI mitigation should be considered from the beginning of design
Interoperability testing with diverse devices is essential
commonPitfalls: Inadequate FOD testing leading to certification failures or safety issues
Using copper wire instead of Litz wire, causing excessive AC losses
Insufficient EMI filtering causing regulatory compliance issues
Poor coil shielding affecting nearby electronics
Inadequate thermal design causing power derating
bestPractices: Conduct comprehensive FOD testing with various metallic objects
Use Litz wire for transmitter and receiver coils
Implement proper EMI filtering and shielding from the start
Design adequate thermal management for continuous operation
Plan for Qi certification early with validation testing
faeInsights.content: Based on years of experience, this Wireless Charging Design Guide article addresses common challenges engineers face in project development.
faeInsights.insightLogic: Recommendations from analysis of successful product deployment projects across industries.

customerCases.problem: The customer was developing their first wireless charging product and struggled with FOD calibration. Their prototype either failed to detect foreign objects or falsely triggered during normal charging, depending on threshold settings.
customerCases.diagnosis: Analysis showed the FOD calibration was not accounting for the specific metallic components in their product's housing. The baseline power loss measurement was incorrect, leading to improper threshold settings.
customerCases.solution: We provided detailed FOD calibration procedures specific to their mechanical design. Our FAE team guided them through baseline measurement, object testing, and threshold optimization. We also recommended minor mechanical modifications to reduce metallic interference.
results: Successfully detected all test foreign objects without false triggers
Passed Qi certification on first submission
Achieved 78% end-to-end efficiency
Product received positive reviews for reliability
Customer launched additional wireless charging products
customerCases.customerFeedback: LiTong's FOD expertise was crucial to our success. The calibration process seemed daunting at first, but their step-by-step guidance made it manageable. Our product has proven very reliable in the field with no FOD-related issues.

faqs.question: What is the difference between wireless charging and wired charging?
faqs.answer: Wireless charging uses magnetic induction to transfer power through the air, while wired charging uses direct electrical connection. Wireless charging offers convenience (no connector wear, easy placement) but typically has lower efficiency (70-80% vs 90-95% for wired) and slower charging speeds. Wired charging provides higher power, better efficiency, and lower cost. Wireless charging is ideal for convenience-focused applications like charging pads, while wired charging remains preferred for high-power and cost-sensitive applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose wireless for convenience and user experience. Use wired for highest efficiency and lowest cost.

faqs.question: How efficient is wireless charging?
faqs.answer: Wireless charging efficiency typically ranges from 70-80% end-to-end, compared to 90-95% for wired charging. Efficiency depends on coil alignment, distance between coils, coil quality (Q factor), and power level. Better alignment and closer coupling improve efficiency. Higher power levels (EPP) are generally more efficient than low power (BPP) due to reduced relative fixed losses. The efficiency loss manifests as heat in both transmitter and receiver.
faqs.decisionGuide: Expect 70-80% efficiency. Design for thermal management to handle the efficiency loss as heat.

faqs.question: What is the maximum power for wireless charging?
faqs.answer: The Qi standard currently supports up to 15W for consumer devices (EPP). Some proprietary implementations offer higher power (up to 40-65W), but these are not Qi-compliant and only work with specific devices. For universal compatibility, design for 15W EPP maximum. Higher power wireless charging requires larger coils, better thermal management, and more sophisticated control. The industry is working on extending Qi standards to higher power levels.
faqs.decisionGuide: Design for 15W maximum for Qi compliance. Consider proprietary solutions only for closed ecosystems.

faqs.question: Does wireless charging work through phone cases?
faqs.answer: Wireless charging works through most phone cases up to 3-4mm thickness. Cases thicker than 4mm may significantly reduce efficiency or prevent charging entirely. Metal cases block wireless charging completely. Cases with metal plates (for magnetic mounts) can interfere with charging. Leather, plastic, and silicone cases generally work fine. Some cases have designated alignment marks for optimal charging position.
faqs.decisionGuide: Design for typical case thickness up to 3mm. Consider case compatibility in product documentation.

faqs.question: What causes wireless charging to stop unexpectedly?
faqs.answer: Unexpected charging interruption can be caused by: Foreign object detection triggering (metallic objects nearby), overheating protection activation, poor coil alignment causing communication loss, incompatible device or protocol, power adapter insufficient for requested power, or fault in transmitter or receiver. Check for these issues systematically. The IP6808 provides status indicators that can help diagnose the cause.
faqs.decisionGuide: Check alignment, remove nearby metal objects, ensure adequate power supply, and monitor temperature.

faqs.question: How do I position devices for optimal wireless charging?
faqs.answer: For optimal wireless charging: Center the device on the charging pad, align the device coil with the transmitter coil (usually centered on device back), remove thick cases if charging is slow, avoid metal objects between device and pad, and ensure the device is properly seated without movement. Some chargers have alignment guides or multiple coils to improve positioning tolerance. LED indicators typically show when proper alignment is achieved.
faqs.decisionGuide: Design charging pads with visual alignment guides. Consider multi-coil designs for better positioning tolerance.
